other_observers: I saw the bird with Andy Boyce and Andrew Spencer, both of whom had better looks than I did.; Andy flushed the bird, and I was the first to ID it.
specific_location: The "2005 grove," at the confluence of drainages just east of the state line on the shelf above the east face of Hoy Mountain, above the grove where the birds were first found in 1988, and south of the grove where they were found in 1991.
description: Andy Boyce flushed a grouse that flew about 30 feet and came back down to the ground. The bird looked smaller than the Dusky Grouse we'd seen earlier in the day. Also seemed to have quieter wingbeats. Bird flushed to base of Douglas-Firs about 10 meters from me to the southeast or east, pretty much 90 degrees left of the sun, but in full shade. Through a gap in the trees I could see the bird from the neck up. It was nervous, bobbing its head rapidly, a behavior we hadn't seen from Dusky Grouse that day, even ones we'd flushed multiple times. It had a brown cast to its plumage and a long cardinal-like crest, very different from the rounded, scruffy crests of Dusky Grouse seen well over the past two days. Bird was on the ground for about 90 seconds before flushing again without apparent provocation. When the bird flushed again, I marveled at how little noise the wings made; at 12 meters, it was barely audible. By contrast, Dusky Grouse wing flushing noise was audible at 30 meters or more.Behaviors: See above.Call: Bird was silent, though see comments below on wing noise.Plumage: adult
